questions.answer: B. The program prints 10 as output
questions.to_find: The output printed by the program

understand.plain: The program starts with a=6 and b=0. Inside the while loop, a is first set to a/12+1 (integer division), then b is incremented, then a is set equal to b. We need to trace each iteration and find when the loop terminates and what value gets printed.

keywords.explain: In C, dividing two integers truncates (floors) the result. So 6/12 = 0, 9/12 = 0, etc.
After each iteration, a equals b (since a=b is the last statement), and b increments by 1 each time.
keywords.example: After iteration k, a = b = k

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="420" height="240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="0" width="420" height="240" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="10" y="22" class="lbl ink">Loop Trace: a starts=6, b starts=0</text><rect x="10" y="30" width="400" height="24" class="bg"/><text x="14" y="47" class="tag mut">Iter | a before | a=a/12+1 | b=b+1 | a=b (end)</text><rect x="10" y="56" width="400" height="20" class="bg"/><text x="14" y="71" class="sub ink">1:      6          1         1       1</text><rect x="10" y="78" width="400" height="20" class="bg"/><text x="14" y="93" class="sub ink">2:      1          1         2       2</text><rect x="10" y="100" width="400" height="20" class="bg"/><text x="14" y="115" class="sub ink">3:      2          1         3       3</text><text x="14" y="138" class="sub mut">... (pattern: a=b increments by 1 each iter)</text><rect x="10" y="148" width="400" height="20" class="bg"/><text x="14" y="163" class="sub ink">9:      9          1        10      10</text><rect x="10" y="170" width="400" height="24" class="hi"/><text x="14" y="187" class="tag ink">After iter 9: a=10, 10 < 10 is FALSE → exit</text><rect x="10" y="200" width="400" height="30" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="14" y="220" class="lbl ink">printf prints: 10</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A table showing each loop iteration: a starts at 6, after a=a/12+1 it becomes 1, then b increments to 1, then a=b=1. Each subsequent iteration b grows by 1 and a=b, so after 9 iterations b=10, a=10, and 10<10 is false so the loop exits printing 10.

given.aim: Determine the output of the program with the while loop and integer arithmetic

terms.meaning: Initial values before the loop starts
terms.example: Starting state of the two variables
terms.connects: First iteration: a/12 = 0 by integer division

terms.meaning: Integer division of current a by 12, then add 1. For any a < 12, this gives 1.
terms.connects: This resets a to 1 every iteration (since a is always < 12 when loop runs)

terms.meaning: b increments by 1 each iteration, then a is assigned the value of b
terms.example: After iteration k: b=k and a=b=k
terms.connects: This is the real progress — a grows via b, one step per iteration

given.plan: Trace the loop iteration by iteration. Key insight: a/12 is always 0 (since a < 12 inside the loop), so a = 0+1 = 1 after the first line. Then b increments by 1 and a=b. So effectively a increases by 1 each iteration. Starting from b=0, after k iterations a=b=k. The loop exits when a >= 10, i.e., when b reaches 10.

steps.title: Simplify the loop body
steps.apply: Since the loop condition is a < 10, inside the loop a is always < 10 < 12. So a/12 = 0 (integer division). Therefore a = 0 + 1 = 1 after the first line, regardless of a's previous value.
steps.note: The first statement a = a/12+1 effectively always sets a to 1 for any a in [0,11].

steps.title: Trace through iterations
steps.apply: Iteration 1: a=6 (enter), a=1, b=1, a=1. Iteration 2: a=1<10, a=1, b=2, a=2. Iteration 3: a=2<10, a=1, b=3, a=3. ... Iteration k: a=k-1<10 (for k<=9), a=1, b=k, a=k. Iteration 9: a=8<10, a=1, b=9, a=9. Iteration 10: a=9<10, a=1, b=10, a=10.
steps.note: After the 10th iteration a=10, which fails the condition a<10, so the loop exits.

steps.title: Read the output
steps.apply: After the loop exits, a = 10. The printf statement prints 10.
steps.note: The loop runs exactly 10 iterations (from b=0 to b=10).

solution.result: The program prints 10 as output. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Integer division truncation in C: for 0 <= a < 12, a/12 = 0. Loop invariant: after each iteration a = b, and b increments by 1 per iteration.

questions.answer: C. The program will terminate with 4321 as output
questions.to_find: The output of the recursive character-reading function given input '1234\n'

understand.plain: The function fX reads one character. If it is not a newline, it calls itself recursively (so it keeps reading characters). After returning from the recursive call, it prints the character if it is not a newline. Because recursion unwinds in reverse order, the characters are printed in reverse.

keywords.explain: Each call to fX is pushed on the call stack. The deepest call returns first, then the one above it, etc. — so actions after the recursive call execute in reverse order.
keywords.example: If fX reads '1','2','3','4','\n', printing happens in order '4','3','2','1'.

keywords.explain: Reads one character from standard input (stdin) each time it is called.
keywords.example: First call reads '1', second reads '2', etc.

<svg width="420" height="300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="0" y="0" width="420" height="300" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="10" y="22" class="lbl ink">fX() Call Stack (input: 1 2 3 4

' → recurse ↓ ... then putchar('1')</text><rect x="40" y="72" width="360" height="36" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="50" y="90" class="sub ink">fX#2: a='2', '2'!='

', condition false → no recurse, no putchar</text><text x="10" y="240" class="tag mut">Unwinding (printing): putchar('4') putchar('3') putchar('2') putchar('1')</text><rect x="10" y="252" width="400" height="36" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="275" class="lbl ink">Output: 4321</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A call stack diagram showing fX called 5 times. fX#1 reads '1', fX#2 reads '2', fX#3 reads '3', fX#4 reads '4', fX#5 reads newline and returns immediately without printing. As stack unwinds: fX#4 prints '4', fX#3 prints '3', fX#2 prints '2', fX#1 prints '1'. Final output is 4321.

given.aim: Trace the recursive function fX with input '1234\n' to find the output

terms.meaning: Read a char into a; if it is not newline, recurse. This drives the recursion deeper until newline is read.
terms.example: Reading '1': not newline, so call fX() again
terms.connects: Recursion continues until newline is encountered — 5 stack frames for input '1234\n'

terms.meaning: After the recursive call returns, print a if it is not a newline. This executes on the way back up the stack.
terms.example: The frame that read '4' will print '4' after the deeper frame (newline) returns
terms.connects: Because printing happens AFTER the recursive call, the stack unwind reverses the print order

terms.meaning: Function calls return in Last-In-First-Out order: the deepest call returns first, so the last character read is the first one printed.
terms.connects: This is the mechanism that reverses the output

given.plan: Trace the recursion depth. For each character read that is not newline, fX recurses before printing. The newline frame returns without printing. Then frames unwind printing characters in reverse: '4','3','2','1'.

steps.title: Build the recursion chain
steps.apply:
fX#1 reads '1' (not '
') → calls fX#2. fX#2 reads '2' (not '
') → calls fX#3. fX#3 reads '3' (not '
') → calls fX#4. fX#4 reads '4' (not '
') → calls fX#5. fX#5 reads '
' → condition false, no recursion, no putchar. fX#5 returns.
steps.note: The recursion depth equals the number of non-newline characters. Here depth = 4.

steps.title: Unwind the stack and print
steps.apply: fX#5 returns → fX#4 resumes: a='4' != '\n' → putchar('4') → prints '4'. fX#4 returns → fX#3 resumes: a='3' → putchar('3') → prints '3'. fX#3 returns → fX#2 resumes: a='2' → putchar('2') → prints '2'. fX#2 returns → fX#1 resumes: a='1' → putchar('1') → prints '1'.
steps.note: Each frame's local variable a still holds the character read during that frame's getchar() call.

steps.title: Combine the output
steps.apply: Characters printed in order: '4', '3', '2', '1'. The program terminates normally after all frames return.
steps.note: The program does terminate because the newline character stops the recursion at finite depth.

solution.result: The program terminates and prints 4321. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Recursive function with post-order action: printing after recursive call reverses the sequence (analogous to printing a linked list in reverse using recursion).
